topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt cold and heavy in Maya's palm, a stark contrast to the warm, dusty air of the entryway. She smoothed the fabric of her borrowed cardigan, acutely aware of the way her worn sneakers looked against the polished oak floor. Grandma Elara’s house was enormous, a sprawling monument to faded elegance, and Maya felt suddenly very small standing in its echoing foyer. Every glance she caught in the long, antique mirrors seemed to linger, assessing her unfamiliar presence. She quickly averted her gaze, focusing instead on the intricate carving of the banister, trying to appear absorbed in the woodwork rather than standing awkwardly by the door. The weight of expectation, inherited along with the deed, pressed down on her shoulders, making her breathe shallowly.

[story 2]

Liam shifted his weight from one foot to the other, the unfamiliar silence of the ancestral home amplifying the sound of his own nervous shuffling. He kept his hands shoved deep into his pockets, a defensive posture against the vastness of the drawing-room. He kept running his eyes over the room—the heavy velvet drapes, the tarnished silver—as if cataloging every imperfection, terrified that any misstep would reveal some inadequacy. "Well," he managed, his voice coming out a little too high, "it certainly has character." He immediately regretted the comment, the sound echoing hollowly. He wanted to disappear into the shadows of the library, wishing the floorboards would swallow him whole.

[story 3]

Clara meticulously adjusted the strap of her small suitcase, her movements jerky and precise. She kept brushing imaginary dust from the sleeve of her simple dress, her eyes darting toward the grand staircase, then immediately snapping back to the hallway. The sheer scale of the house made her feel exposed, like an exhibit under bright lights. When she finally dared to look up, she found herself staring at her own reflection in a tall, gilt-framed mirror—a stranger with wide, uncertain eyes. A tightness constricted her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. She needed a task, something concrete to occupy her hands, anything to distract from the unsettling feeling of being scrutinized by the silent, imposing architecture.

She smoothed the fabric of her borrowed cardigan, acutely aware of the way her worn sneakers looked against the polished oak floor. Liam shifted his weight from one foot to the other, the unfamiliar silence of the ancestral home amplifying the sound of his own nervous shuffling. He kept his hands shoved deep into his pockets, a defensive posture against the vastness of the drawing-room. He kept running his eyes over the room—the heavy velvet drapes, the tarnished silver—as if cataloging every imperfection, terrified that any misstep would reveal some inadequacy. Clara meticulously adjusted the strap of her small suitcase, her movements jerky and precise. The sheer scale of the house made her feel exposed, like an exhibit under bright lights.
